summary: DHH describes the intellectual thrill of changing long-held beliefs, using his shift on nuclear power as the primary example. Drawing on Michael Shellenberger's arguments, he outlines why wind and solar alone cannot solve climate change due to reliability, storage, and ecological issues. He makes the case that nuclear power is a superior energy source when trade-offs are weighed objectively, noting that historical opposition from environmentalists now looks like a catastrophic error. The post ultimately argues that progress on any topic requires willingness to revisit assumptions and embrace better arguments over tribal loyalties.
key_points: Wind and solar are unreliable and require dirty backup sources like coal, making exclusive reliance on them counterproductive
Nuclear power emits no greenhouse gases, provides abundant predictable power, and has caused far fewer deaths than commonly feared
The spectacle of nuclear disasters like Chernobyl and Fukushima has massively overstated their risks relative to everyday energy sources
Germany serves as a cautionary tale of over-investing in renewables while becoming dependent on Russian natural gas
Intellectual progress requires the willingness to change your mind when presented with better arguments and data
money_quotes: I've changed my mind on a lot of topics over the last few years, and it's frankly been exhilarating.
If you over-invest in wind and solar, you may end up with a power grid that's simultaneously expensive, unreliable, dirty, and without sovereignty.
The nuclear waste generated can be stored on-site, and the waste needed for a single person's lifetime energy consumption can fit in a coke can.
Their impact has largely been drawn from the spectacle of disaster. And as a result been massively overstated.
When we weigh the trade offs objectively, nuclear emerges as shining star. Not because there are no risks, but because they are far less than the alternatives.
The historical opposition from environmentalists now looks like a catastrophic error in hindsight.
Fall in love with the thrill of accepting the better argument – in contrast to mere social pressure – and you'll help pave the path to that better world.
key_insight: Progress comes not from grand solutions but from the willingness to change your mind when better arguments emerge, trading tribal loyalty for honest evaluation of trade-offs.
